record_office_terminology.marriage_licenses: Register of Deeds (post-1868); prior records in county court minutes, church records
record_office_terminology.vital_records_state_start: 1913 (births and deaths statewide registration); marriages recorded at county level from colonial era

burned_county_authority: FamilySearch Burned Counties Research wiki; NC State Archives county records inventories; Helen Leary, North Carolina Research: Genealogy and Local History (2nd ed.)
administrative_notes: North Carolina's 100 counties were formed over 250+ years, with the earliest (Albemarle/Bath precincts) dating to the 1660s-1700s. The state lost significant records in the Civil War (1865) and through individual courthouse fires spanning 1830-1921. NC uses Register of Deeds for land records and Clerk of Superior Court for probate/estates. URLs in this file should be verified before production use.

core_repositories.repository: NC Department of Natural and Cultural Resources
core_repositories.description: Primary state archive. Houses colonial, state, and county records including wills, deeds, estate papers, court records, military records, and vital records. Search Room open for in-person research. Extensive microfilm collection.

core_repositories.repository: NC Department of Natural and Cultural Resources
core_repositories.description: Digitized documents, photographs, maps, and publications from NC state agencies, libraries, and archives. Includes county records, historical maps, and government publications.

core_repositories.source_name: State Library of North Carolina — Genealogy Resources
core_repositories.description: Genealogy research guides, county record inventories, and links to online resources. Houses extensive collection of county histories, family histories, and genealogical periodicals.
Volunteer-maintained county-level genealogy pages with transcribed records, cemetery listings, and research guides for all 100 NC counties.

familysearch_collections.description: Extensive NC microfilm and digitized records. Includes county-level deed, will, marriage, estate, and court records. Many collections indexed and name-searchable. API access available for most record types.
Consolidated list of all online NC genealogy records available through FamilySearch, including direct links to digitized collections.

newspapers_and_periodicals.repository: Library of Congress / National Digital Newspaper Program
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Digitized and searchable North Carolina newspaper titles. Free full-text search. Part of the National Digital Newspaper Program.

newspapers_and_periodicals.repository: North Carolina Digital Heritage Center / UNC Chapel Hill
newspapers_and_periodicals.description: Free access to digitized historic newspapers from communities across North Carolina. Includes hundreds of titles from the 1700s through the 2000s.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: NC State Archives — African American Records
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Slave records, free person of color records, apprentice bonds, Freedmen's Bureau records, cohabitation records (1866 — formerly enslaved couples registering marriages). Critical for post-Civil War family reconstruction.

enslaved_persons_and_african_american.source_name: FamilySearch — NC Freedmen's Bureau Field Office Records
enslaved_persons_and_african_american.description: Bureau of Refugees, Freedmen, and Abandoned Lands records from NC field offices (1865-1872). Includes labor contracts, marriage records, school reports, complaints, and ration records. Often includes names of former slaveholders.

Under an 1866 NC law, formerly enslaved couples were required to register their marriages. These cohabitation records often name both partners, children, former slaveholder, and plantation. Surviving records exist for approximately 40 counties. One of the most important sources for identifying enslaved families.

Interviews with formerly enslaved persons in North Carolina conducted 1936-1938. Part of the Federal Writers' Project. Includes personal accounts, family histories, and descriptions of plantation life.

native_american_and_removal_records.source_name: Eastern Band of Cherokee Indians Records
native_american_and_removal_records.repository: Museum of the Cherokee Indian / Western Carolina University
native_american_and_removal_records.description: The Eastern Band of Cherokee Indians is based in Qualla Boundary, Swain and Jackson Counties. Key records include the Baker Roll (1924), Cherokee census rolls, and records at the Museum of the Cherokee Indian in Cherokee, NC. Western NC counties (Cherokee, Clay, Graham, Jackson, Macon, Swain) are primary research areas.

native_american_and_removal_records.repository: Lumbee Tribe of North Carolina / UNC Pembroke
native_american_and_removal_records.description: The Lumbee Tribe, based in Robeson County, is the largest tribe east of the Mississippi. Research centers on Robeson, Hoke, Scotland, and Cumberland Counties. UNC Pembroke maintains the North Carolina Collection and Native American Resource Center. State-recognized since 1885, federally recognized by the Lumbee Act of 1956 (partial).
Eastern Cherokee census rolls covering 1835-1884. Includes the 1835 Henderson Roll, 1848 Mullay Roll, 1851 Siler Roll, and 1884 Hester Roll. Critical for documenting Cherokee ancestry in western NC.

military_records.description: Revolutionary War records, War of 1812 records, Civil War records (both Union and Confederate NC troops), WWI and WWII records. Includes muster rolls, pension applications, service records, and correspondence.

military_records.source_name: Roster of NC Troops in the War Between the States (Clark)
military_records.description: Walter Clark's 5-volume roster of North Carolina troops 1861-1865. Includes unit histories and individual soldier records. NC provided more Confederate troops than any other state.

land_and_property.source_name: NC Secretary of State — Land Grants
land_and_property.description: NC land grants from the colonial and early statehood periods. The Secretary of State holds original land grant records. NC's land grant system differs from federal public domain states.

land_and_property.source_name: NC Register of Deeds Association — County Directory
land_and_property.repository: NC Association of Register of Deeds
land_and_property.description: Directory of all 100 NC county Register of Deeds offices with links to online deed search portals where available. Many counties now offer online deed searches through various vendors.

vital_records.repository: NC Department of Health and Human Services
vital_records.description: NC began statewide birth and death registration in 1913. Pre-1913 vital events are found in county records, church records, Bible records, and cemetery records. Marriage records kept at county level (Register of Deeds) from colonial era.

church_and_cemetery.description: Cemetery transcriptions available through NCGenWeb county pages, Find A Grave, and BillionGraves. NC has extensive rural church cemeteries dating to the colonial era, particularly Moravian (Forsyth/Wachovia), Quaker (Guilford/Randolph), and Scotch-Irish Presbyterian (western Piedmont) records.

research_guides_and_societies.description: Statewide genealogical society. Publishes NC Genealogical Society Journal (quarterly). Offers workshops, webinars, and research assistance.

research_guides_and_societies.source_name: Helen Leary — North Carolina Research: Genealogy and Local History (2nd ed.)
research_guides_and_societies.description: Definitive reference guide for NC genealogical research. Covers record types, county formations, burned counties, and research strategies. Essential companion for any serious NC research.

historical_counties_extinct.description: North Carolina's 100 counties were formed over 250+ years. The earliest precinct/county (Albemarle) dates to the 1660s. Major formation periods: colonial precincts (1660s-1730s), early statehood (1777-1800), and final wave (1800-1911, ending with Avery County). Several extinct counties include: Archdale (1705-1712), Bath (1696-1739), Clarendon (1664-1667), Dobbs (1758-1791, split into Wayne and Glasgow/Lenoir). Researchers should trace county formations to identify where records may be filed under predecessor county names.

burned_counties_summary.authority: FamilySearch Burned Counties Research wiki; NC State Archives county records inventories; Helen Leary, North Carolina Research (2nd ed.)

counties.losses: Civil War era destruction. Partial loss of county records.
Records damaged/destroyed during Civil War Union occupation of Washington, NC.
1800 courthouse fire destroyed early records; 1893 fire destroyed additional records.
Civil War era destruction. Significant loss of antebellum records.
1830 courthouse fire; additional losses during Civil War.
Records lost in 1865 and 1895 courthouse fires.
Sherman's troops damaged records; some records survived.
Civil War era destruction. Partial loss of records.
Union forces occupied county seat; records damaged.
Two fires destroyed early colonial records.
1865 loss of court records; 1921 clerk office fire destroyed additional records.
